---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size75[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size45[
What are the Complicating Factors in Environmental Policymaking?

+ Collective Action Problems and Free-Riding
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Core201 FA17 7-1
2. Prep email with link to Google sheet

<br>

Today we discuss our next complication for environmental problem-solving: collective action problems

What do I mean by a collective action problem?

Let's start with the first sentence from the Wikipedia entry!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size55["A collective action problem or social dilemma is a situation in which all individuals would be better off cooperating but fail to do so because of conflicting interests between individuals that discourage joint action" (Wikipedia 2021).]

???

### Anybody take a stab at explaining this in english?

<br>

On second thought, maybe this still isn't very clear.

### Has anybody ever heard of the prisoner's dilemma?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-bank_robbery.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

You can tell the story many different ways, but here's a quick version.

Two thieves rob a bank, then split up as they leave with a plan to meet up later and divvy up the loot.

Cops catch each of them separately and bring them "downtown" for questioning.

Both still had their guns and masks with them when captured so are in some trouble / look pretty darn suspicious.

+ e.g. gun possession is good for a couple years in jail

+ The masks start to build a case a jury might convict on.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-interrogation.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Now we introduce the dilemma and why the cops are in such a strong position once they capture you.

The robbers are placed in separate interrogation rooms.

Each is made an offer at essentially the same time.

+ The first one to confess to the robbery and agree to testify against the other goes free.

<br>

Put yourself in the shoes of one of the robbers.

As a first pass, we can think of this as a simple decision for you.

(SLIDE)


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-cartoon_thief.webp')
background-size: 60%
background-position: left
class: middle

.pull-right[
.size55[**Your Decision**]

.size45[
+ Confess quickly and go free (maybe)

+ Stay silent and go to jail
]]

???

In a world where only you matter, the decision is pretty easy to think through as a cost-benefit analysis calculation.

<br>

However, the outcome of this dilemma depends on both the choice YOU make AND the choice made by the other suspect.

<br>

That means, it's time for some game theory!

Game Theory is a theoretical approach to analyzing decision-making born from micro-economics.

It is designed for situations in which the outcomes depend on the interactions of choices made by two or more actors.



---

class: middle, center

.size50[.center[.content-box-blue[**The Prisoner's Dilemma**]]]

<br>

<br>

```{css, echo=F}
/* Change the background color to white for shaded rows (even rows) */
.remark-slide thead, .remark-slide tr:nth-child(2n) {
      background-color: white;
}
```

```{r}
tibble(
  col1 = c("You", ""),
  col2 = c("Silence", "Confess"),
  Cooperate = c("2 years, 2 years", "Go Free, 10 years"),
  Defect = c("10 years, Go Free", "6 years, 6 years")
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c("l", "l", "c", "c"), col.names = c("", "", "Silence", "Confess")) |>
  kableExtra::add_header_above(c(" " = 2, "Other Suspect" = 2)) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 1:2, bold = TRUE, width = "10em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 3:4, background = "#b3ccff", width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 35, bootstrap_options = "basic")
```

???

This is what we call a non-cooperative game in normal form.

The four boxes represent the four possible outcomes of the game

We get to a single box once we know the decision of each player.

<br>

*Go through a couple to make sure it's clear*

<br>

### So, in this position what would you do and why?

*Force discussion of each option*

<br>

### What pieces of context would influence how you think about the decision? 

(e.g. other suspect your best friend or just some guy? How much evidence do the cops already have? Do you already have a record?)

<br>

So, how does game theory help us with this?


--

<br>

<br>

.center[.size35[.content-box-red[**What is the most likely outcome of the game?**]]]

???

Game theory concepts like Pareto optimality and the Nash Equilibrium can help us predict the most likely outcomes and the "best" strategies for each player.

In other words, game theory tries to "solve" games like this one.




---

class: middle, center

.size50[.center[.content-box-blue[**The Prisoner's Dilemma**]]]

<br>

<br>

```{css, echo=F}
/* Change the background color to white for shaded rows (even rows) */
.remark-slide thead, .remark-slide tr:nth-child(2n) {
      background-color: white;
}
```

```{r}
tibble(
  col1 = c("You", ""),
  col2 = c("Silence", "Confess"),
  Cooperate = c("2 years", "Go Free"),
  Defect = c("10 years", "6 years")
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c("l", "l", "c", "c"), col.names = c("", "", "Silence", "Confess")) |>
  kableExtra::add_header_above(c(" " = 2, "Other Suspect" = 2)) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 1:2, bold = TRUE, width = "10em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 3:4, background = "#b3ccff", width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 35, bootstrap_options = "basic")
```

???

Finding the Nash Equilibrium means thinking about your choice as if  you had perfect information.

- e.g. You know what the other robber is going to do before he does it!

<br>

### So, if you knew for certain the other robber would stay silent, what would you do?

#### + Would you pick 2 years in jail or to go free? 

*Think of your elderly parents and 16 children!*

<br>

### On the other hand, what if you knew for certain they were going to confess, what would you choose?

#### + What would you do if you knew they were going to sell your ass out?

<br>

If the answer in both circumstances is to confess, then confession is what we call your "dominant" strategy.

The dominant strategy in game theory refers to a situation where one player has a superior tactic regardless of how the other players act. 

No matter what the other person does, your better outcome comes from confessing.



---

class: middle, center

.size50[.center[.content-box-blue[**The Prisoner's Dilemma**]]]

<br>

<br>

```{css, echo=F}
/* Change the background color to white for shaded rows (even rows) */
.remark-slide thead, .remark-slide tr:nth-child(2n) {
      background-color: white;
}
```

```{r}
tibble(
  col1 = c("You", ""),
  col2 = c("Silence", "Confess"),
  Cooperate = c("2 years, 2 years", "Go Free, 10 years"),
  Defect = c("10 years, Go Free", "6 years, 6 years")
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c("l", "l", "c", "c"), col.names = c("", "", "Silence", "Confess")) |>
  kableExtra::add_header_above(c(" " = 2, "Other Suspect" = 2)) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 1:2, bold = TRUE, width = "10em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 3, background = "#b3ccff", width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 4, background = c("#b3ccff", "red"), width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 35, bootstrap_options = "basic")
```

???

It turns out the logic is mirrored for both suspects.

For both players, confessing is the dominant strategy.

If both sides are better off confessing then we end up in the bottom right outcome.

<br>

Since both dominant strategies lead to only one outcome, that outcome is called the Nash Equilibrium.

Given the structure of this game and its uncertainty, both robbers should choose to confess regardless of what the other does.

### Make sense?

Now, back to the definition!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size55["A collective action problem or social dilemma is a situation in which all individuals would be better off cooperating but fail to do so because of conflicting interests between individuals that discourage joint action" (Wikipedia 2021).]

???

### Now do we have a better sense of what this definition is talking about?

#### - How does our PD game illustrate the collective action problem?

Collective action problems arise when individuals want to cooperate but can't seem to get there because of the complexities of the world.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size55[
Hardin, G. (1968). The Tragedy of the Commons. *Science*. 162(3859), 1243–1248.
]

???

For today I had you read a short excerpt from Garret Hardin’s "Tragedy of the Commons."

#### - Everybody have the reading?

#### - Anybody ever read this before?

<br>

First and formeost let me tell you, I AGONIZED over whether or not to assign any piece of this reading to you.

On the plus side this is one of, if not THE, most cited and assigned research articles studying policy and the environment.

- Nearly 50,000 citations.

The problem is...



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-tragedy_of_the_tragedy.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

Hardin's environmental concerns were driven almost entirely by his racism and disdain for the poor.

I assigned you only the portion relevant to our work, that which describes the pasture thought experiment.

The rest of the article is scary and we'll talk more about that part of it next week when we explore how inequality complicates environmental problem-solving.

SO, hold off on that piece of this article!

<br>

Ok, let's talk about the "commons" concept here.

### Can anybody define the "commons" part of the "tragedy of the commons"?

(SLIDE)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-The_Ocean.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: bottom

.center[
.size30[
.content-box-blue["...the natural resources and vital life-support services that]

.content-box-blue[belong to all humankind rather than to any one country"]

.content-box-blue[(Chasek, Downie and Brown 2014)]
]]

???

Here is the definition of a commons as provided by Oran Young.

### What specific resources does this definition refer to?

Think oceans, seabed minerals, Antarctica and the atmosphere.

None exist within the borders of any sovereign state.

### Any questions on the baseline concept?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-empty_pasture.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

The tragedy of the commons is typically explained using a story about a pasture.

### Who owns this pasture?

(- No one?)
(- Everyone?)
(- Both?)

### Does it make sense that if no one owns the pasture, everyone owns it?

#### - Does that equivalency make sense?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-pasture.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

For the sake of this discussion, all of you are ranchers who own herds of cattle.

On this commons you all choose to graze your cattle.

### Why would you choose to graze your cattle on the commons pasture and not your own land?

(maximize benefits by not having to pay to feed them!)

<br>

So, let's go back to the problematic equivalency from a moment ago.

### Who actually pays to feed your cattle?

+ (Everyone)
+ (plus the environment and the public)



---

class: middle, slidegreen

.size50[.center[**Cost-Benefit Analysis: Add a Cow**]]

.pull-left[
```{r, fig.retina=3}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/07-1-Cow.webp')
```
]

???

Let's put this in the language of simple cost-benefit analysis.

We should expect each of you to add a cow to your herd when the benefits outweigh the costs.

### What are the benefits of adding a cow to your herd?
(Milk or meat, reproduction, friendship...)

### What are the costs?
(Feed, care, housing)


--

.pull-right[

<br>

<br>

## To Your Farm
.size40[
+ Benefits = +1

+ Costs = -1
]]

???

To keep things simple, let's say each of you currently face the following decision.

The hypothetical costs and benefits of adding a cow to your herd is almost perfectly off-setting. 

### In this instance, should you add a cow to your herd? Why or why not?

<br>

(Probably not in this case)

(Maybe if as your scale increases the costs of adding each additional cow falls?)

<br>

Now, what happens to the CBA when we add a cow to the commons?

### How does using the commons to feed your new cow alter your CBA?

(SLIDE)




---

class: middle, slidegreen

.size50[.center[**Cost-Benefit Analysis: Add a Cow**]]

.pull-left[
```{r, fig.retina=3}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/07-1-Cow.webp')
```
]

.pull-right[

<br>

<br>

## To the Commons
.size40[
+ Benefits = +1

+ Costs = -1/x
]]

???

### Why don't the benefits change?

<br>

### Explain the 1/x to me in the costs of adding a cow to the commons.

(X is the population size)

- Costs shared amongst everyone
- The commons loses 1, but that cost is divided amongst the owners of the commons e.g. everybody.

### Does that make sense?

Important distinction here: Society pays MOST of the costs of feeding your cattle.

- That means we each pay part of the cost.

<br>

### So, if you are the only person around, should you add a cow to the commons?

(Takes us back to the previous +1 + -1 = 0.)

<br>

### What happens to the costs you face as the population around you gets bigger?

(Your share of the cost shrinks to almost nothing)

- The more of us there are, the smaller your individual share.

<br>

### So, what is the bottom line here? when should you add a cow to your herd?

+ (When there is a commons to feed it)
+ (When there are a lot of other people also using it)

### And what does all of this mean for the survival of the commons?

(SLIDE)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-ruined_pasture.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

It's toast in the long-run.

### Why does Hardin refer to this as a tragedy? Is it because he’s an environmentalist and the story is sad?

--

.center[.size35[.content-box-blue[The essence of dramatic tragedy is not unhappiness.]

.content-box-blue[It resides in the solemnity of the remorseless]

.content-box-blue[working of things.]]]

???

<br>

Think of the Whitehead quote Hardin invokes 

(p1244)

He's describing this as a dramatic tragedy.

According to Hardin the tragedy is inevitable and unavoidable because of the nature of the commons.

The system is like an engine.

It does what it must based on its design without thought or compunction (e.g. guilt).

<br>

### Why does Hardin believe the ranchers cannot help themselves, even when they know what will happen?

(SLIDE)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-ruined_pasture.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size35[.content-box-blue[1) The benefits are real and immediate]]

<br>

.size35[.content-box-blue[2) The costs are small and shared]]

<br>

.size35[.content-box-blue[3) Even if you do the "right" thing, someone else will]]

.size35[.content-box-blue[add another cow and the commons will collapse]]

???

### Any questions on the tragedy of the commons story?

<br>

The pasture version of this story is very specific to the using of a shared resource.

- e.g. fish from the ocean, trees from a forest, etc.

Let's also note how Hardin extends the logic to pollution.

<br>



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-waste_river.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

### Per Hardin's article, how does this image represent a tragedy of the commons?

#### - What is the commons here?

#### - What is the "use" of the commons?

<br>

(We aren’t removing goods from the pasture, we are dumping pollution into it.)

- Behaving as “independent, rational, free-enterprisers” we are locked into a system of “fouling our own nest”. p1245

<br>

Ok, let's now tie this back to our motivating question.

When do collective action problems complicate environmental problem-solving?


--

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[Benefits to the Individual]]]

<br>

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[+]]]

<br>

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[Costs to the Collective]]]

<br>

.center[.size45[.content-box-red[= Resource Depletion / Destruction]]]


???

The takeaway for us is this.

One serious form of collective action problem that complicates environmental problem-solving involves actions that benefit the individual that, in the aggregate, harm the community.

### Does this make sense?

### Is anybody working on an environmental problem that clearly exhibits this dynamic in action?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-logic_col_action.jpg')
background-size: 40%
background-position: left
class: middle, center, slideblue

.pull-right[
.size45[
Costs to the Individual

+

Benefits to the Collective

=

Free-Riders!
]]

???

Let's jump to our other reading for today.

Mancur Olson identifies that flipping the costs and benefits ALSO leads to a serious problem: Free-riders.

In Hardin's story, individuals make good choices for themselves which combine to make bad choices for society.

In Olson's story, he shows us that it is REALLY hard to get individuals to pay tiny costs in exchange for big benefits for the wider society. 

His focus is on why using groups to achieve change is hard. 

*Seminar style discussion of the arguments in the intro + chap 1*

<br>

### What did you get from the reading?

#### - Any big ideas jump out?

#### - Any parts that made little to no sense we could discuss?

<br>

### What does Olson's argument mean for our theories of democracy that assume individuals need groups in order to achieve change?





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-green_blue_swirl_side.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

.size60[**For Thursday**]

.size40[
Find us a **recent, real world example** of **collective action problems / free-riding** complicating environmental problem-solving.

+ Individual benefits + collective costs, OR
+ Individual costs + collective benefits

Be ready to help us **brainstorm strategies** for addressing these complications.
]

???

You don't have to find a case that mirrors the problem you've selected for your paper.

HOWEVER, that's not a bad idea!

My aim is that we broaden and deepen our understanding of risk profiles complicating environmental problem-solving.

So, go find us good cases we can dig our teeth into.

**Submit the citation to your case before class.**

Be ready to present your case to the class AND start thinking about strategies we can use to address these risk based problems.

### Questions?
